→ Schedule everything

body:
My exact content batching workflow (step by step):

Sunday, 2 hours:
1. Pick the week's 5 content pillars
2. Write 2 X tweets + 1 LinkedIn post per pillar
3. Add hooks, CTAs, UTM links
4. Schedule via Buffer/Hypefury

That's it. 15 posts done before Monday.

I break down this system in detail inside my book: https://buildexlab.gumroad.com/l/SFCS

The fix: batch creation, not daily creation.

One 2-hour Sunday session > 14 rushed weekday sessions.

Your audience gets consistency. You get your evenings back.

hook: 5 hooks that consistently get 1,000+ impressions on X
body:
5 hooks that consistently get 1,000+ impressions on X:

1. "I did X for 30 days. Here's what happened:"
2. "Stop [common thing]. Do this instead:"
3. "[Number] things nobody tells you about [topic]:"
4. "Unpopular opinion: [contrarian take]"
5. "I analyzed [big number]. Every single one had this in common:"

Save this.

hook: The LinkedIn algorithm rewards one thing above all else. Here's what it is:
body:
The LinkedIn algorithm rewards one thing above all else:

Dwell time.

Not likes. Not comments. How long someone reads your post.

This means:
→ Short first line that stops the scroll
→ Line breaks that slow down reading
→ A payoff worth reading to the end

Write posts people finish.

hook: I analyzed 200 viral tweets. Every single one had this in common:
body:
I analyzed 200 viral tweets.

Every single one had this in common: a crystal-clear first line.

Not clever. Not funny (usually). Just clear.

Clear > clever. Every time.

This is one of 12 frameworks inside the Short-Form Content System book: https://buildexlab.gumroad.com/l/SFCS

body:

1. Write a LinkedIn long-form post
2. Pull the best line → X tweet
3. Break it into a 5-tweet thread
4. Turn the thread into a newsletter section
5. Record yourself explaining it → short video

One idea. Five pieces. Done.

Step 2: Extract 2 standalone insights → 2 X tweets.
Step 3: Turn the X tweets into a thread.
Step 4: Summarize the thread into a newsletter tip.

One 45-minute writing session. 5–7 pieces of content. That's leverage.

4. Tweet 8: summarize with the takeaway
5. Last tweet: CTA or question

Structure > inspiration. Every time.

Start optimizing for saves. Everything else follows.

1. Find 3 similar products in your niche
2. Note their prices
3. Price at the midpoint
4. Add a specific outcome to justify it
5. Test for 30 days, then adjust

Don't guess. Price strategically.

5. Respond to every comment on your post

Repeat daily. Community builds itself.

hook: Short vs. long LinkedIn posts: what my 6-month test revealed
body:
Short vs. long LinkedIn posts: what my 6-month test revealed.

Short posts (under 100 words):
→ Higher engagement rate
→ More shares
→ Lower dwell time

Long posts (300–500 words):
→ Higher saves
→ More trust-building
→ Better for product mentions

Conclusion: alternate between both. Use short for reach, long for conversion.

hook: Writing short-form content is a skill. Here's how to practice it:
body:
Writing short-form content is a skill. Here's how to practice it:

1. Write a long post
2. Cut it in half
3. Cut it in half again
4. See if the core idea survived

If it did: that's your post.

If it didn't: the original post was padding.

Grab the Short-Form Content System before the price goes up: https://buildexlab.gumroad.com/l/SFCS

hook: My exact DM template for landing collabs and partnerships
body:
My exact DM template for landing collabs and partnerships:

"Hey [name], been following your work on [specific thing]. I create content on [topic] and think our audiences overlap a lot. Would you be open to a quick swap — I share yours, you share mine?"

Short. Specific. Easy to say yes to.

hook: The creator partnership playbook (how small accounts land big deals)
body:
The creator partnership playbook:

1. Find 10 creators in adjacent niches (not competing)
2. Engage genuinely for 2 weeks
3. Propose a content swap: you post about them, they post about you
4. After the swap, propose a co-created piece
5. Document the partnership publicly — social proof for both

Small accounts can land big deals when they lead with value.

1. Review your notes from the week (3 min)
2. Write 10 hook ideas (5 min)
3. Pick the 7 best → schedule for next week (2 min)

hook: The 3 phases of a high-converting launch thread
body:
The 3 phases of a high-converting launch thread:

Phase 1 (Hooks): problem agitation — make them feel the pain
Phase 2 (Stakes): solution preview — make them want the answer
Phase 3 (CTA): offer with urgency — make them act now

Launch threads aren't ads. They're structured storytelling.

hook: How to launch a digital product to your LinkedIn audience (without being spammy)
body:
How to launch a digital product to your LinkedIn audience (without being spammy):

Week 1: Teach the problem your product solves
Week 2: Show the solution in action
Week 3: Share social proof from early users
Week 4: Make the ask

By week 4, they've already decided. The ask is just permission.

hook: 7 lessons from 1 year of daily content creation
body:
7 lessons from 1 year of daily content creation:

1. Hooks matter more than ideas
2. Consistency beats quality at first
3. Systems outlast motivation
4. Your best content is evergreen
5. Engagement is a skill you develop
6. Small audiences buy more than large ones
7. The money comes after the trust

Year 1 is the hardest. Keep going.

hook: The creator journey: what year 1 really looks like (raw and honest)
body:
The creator journey: what year 1 really looks like.

Month 1: Excitement. Posting daily.
Month 2: Crickets. Questioning everything.
Month 3: First sign of traction. One post performs.
Month 4-6: Slow growth. Learning the algorithm.
Month 7-9: System clicks. Growth accelerates.
Month 10-12: Monetization. First digital product sales.

It's not overnight. It's a year of showing up.

Stay in the game.

hook: The single most important piece of content advice I've ever received
body:
The single most important piece of content advice I've ever received:

"Write for one specific person, not an audience."

When you write for everyone, you reach no one.
When you write for one reader, thousands feel seen.

Who is your one reader?
